questions.question: According to research cited in the lesson, what percentage of legacy projects have formal succession plans, and how much longer do those with succession plans show impact duration?

options.text: About 50% have succession plans, showing 2x longer impact
About 75% have succession plans, showing 1.5x longer impact
Only 23% have formal succession plans, yet those with succession planning show 4.2x longer impact duration
Nearly all (95%) have succession plans, showing 10x longer impact

questions.explanation: Research shows only 23% of legacy projects have formal succession plans—but those that do show 4.2x longer impact duration. The difference between work that dies with you and work that outlives you is usually intentional planning, not luck. Succession isn't morbid or pessimistic; it's the ultimate act of generativity. Best succession planning starts early (not in crisis), involves documenting knowledge, training successors, and gradual transition over years.

questions.question: What percentage of successors benefit from documented knowledge transfer according to the lesson?

options.text: About 35% benefit from documentation
About 88% of successors benefit from documented knowledge transfer
Only 10% find documentation useful
100% require documentation to succeed

questions.explanation: Research shows 88% of successors benefit from documented knowledge transfer. This includes documenting: core frameworks and models (what AND how you teach), decision-making frameworks (when to say yes/no, how you handle ethical dilemmas), relationship networks and resources (key contacts, referral sources), tacit knowledge ('the 70% that's not in books'), failure logs (what doesn't work), and values/philosophy (the WHY behind your work). Documentation helps future successors avoid starting from scratch and prevents repeating your mistakes.

questions.question: What is the 4-stage model for training successors described in the lesson?

options.text: Hire → Train → Test → Launch
Observe (they watch you) → Assist (they help you) → Lead with support (you watch them) → Independent with consultation (they lead, you're available)
Study → Practice → Master → Teach
Interview → Shadow → Replace → Retire

questions.explanation: The 4-stage training model: (1) Observe—successor watches you, reads materials, learns what you do and how; (2) Assist—successor co-facilitates portions, handles logistics, leads exercises while you supervise; (3) Lead with Support—successor leads sessions while you observe and provide feedback, they make decisions and you're safety net; (4) Independent with Consultation—successor operates independently, you're available when they request consultation but not actively involved. This gradual transition happens over years, not overnight, allowing successor to gain confidence while you're still around to help solve problems.

questions.question: Which succession model does NOT rely on finding one person to replace you?

options.text: Single Successor (One-to-One Replacement)
Distributed Leadership (Multiple People, Different Roles) and Peer-Led Community (No Central Leader)
All succession models require finding one replacement
Only Documented Content model

questions.explanation: Multiple succession models exist: (1) Single Successor—one person steps into your role (clear continuity but single point of failure); (2) Distributed Leadership—break your role into components and train different people for different pieces (resilient, no single point of failure); (3) Peer-Led Community—transition from leader-centric to peer support without needing you (most sustainable); (4) Documented Content—create self-serve resources that serve people without direct involvement (infinite scalability). The most robust succession often creates systems enabling multiple people to contribute rather than relying on finding one perfect replacement.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what's a healthy response to the fear 'What if they do it differently?'

options.text: Create strict rules so they must do it exactly your way
They WILL do it differently—and that's good. Your way isn't the only way. Their adaptations may improve it. Control prevents growth; trust enables evolution
Only choose successors who promise to replicate your approach exactly
Don't train successors to avoid this problem

questions.explanation: The healthy response: They WILL do it differently—and that's good. Your way isn't the only way. Their adaptations may improve it, reach different people, or simply reflect their authentic voice. Control prevents growth; trust enables evolution. This is about emotional work of letting go. Other fears addressed: 'What if they fail?' (They might make mistakes like you did—you're responsible for equipping them well, not controlling every decision), 'Who am I without this work?' (Stepping back forces identity reckoning—your worth isn't your work), 'What if no one wants to continue it?' (That's information—maybe it served its purpose).

title: Legacy Planning and Succession Strategies - Quiz
